g.data-narration: Welcome to applications of public key cryptosystems covering digital signatures, key exchange, encryption and authentication
g.text: Digital Signatures • Key Exchange • Encryption • Authentication

g.data-narration: Alice and Bob need to share a secret over an insecure channel without meeting first
g.text: Insecure Channel - Need to share secret without meeting!

g.data-narration: Public key cryptography provides four main applications: digital signatures, key exchange, encryption and authentication
Digital signatures provide authentication, integrity and non-repudiation by signing with private key and verifying with public key
Diffie-Hellman key exchange allows Alice and Bob to establish a shared secret over an insecure channel
g.text: 2. Key Exchange - Establishing Shared Secret Keys
Public Value Exchange (g, p, g^a, g^b)

g.data-narration: For encryption, anyone can encrypt with the public key but only the private key holder can decrypt the message
g.text: 3. Data Encryption (Confidentiality) - Encrypt with Public Key

g.data-narration: User authentication uses challenge-response: server verifies user identity by checking digital signature with public key
g.text: 4. User Authentication - Proving Identity with Private Key

g.data-narration: Hybrid systems combine asymmetric crypto for key exchange with symmetric crypto for fast bulk encryption
g.text: Hybrid Systems - Combining Best of Both Worlds

g.data-narration: Summary table comparing all four applications of public key cryptography with their security goals and key usage
Key takeaways: sign with private key, encrypt with public key, hybrid combines both, foundation of HTTPS
